critic_raw:
The experimental comparison is confounded by a severe feature asymmetry that makes any performance difference uninterpretable. The RNN receives rich temporal sequences of 26 two-week windows per employee, capturing communication dynamics over time. The baseline logistic regression receives only static aggregates — tenure, department, job level, and a handful of communication frequency summaries — with no temporal structure. This means the RNN has categorical access to information the baseline does not. Any observed advantage for the RNN cannot be attributed to the model architecture; it equally reflects the information advantage. A valid comparison would either give LR the same temporal features or give the RNN only the static features.

The stratified random split applied to temporally structured employee data creates temporal leakage. Employee communication patterns evolve continuously; a stratified random split means the model can train on an employee's behavior from month 18 while predicting outcomes based on month 6 data — the opposite of causal ordering. The description briefly notes that temporal boundaries are enforced in the label definition (observation window to T=720, prediction window T=720–810) but the split itself is random across the full dataset duration, allowing exactly the kind of temporal mixing it claims to prevent.

Preprocessing transformers — scaling, tokenization, and imputation — are fit on the combined dataset before splitting. For communication data that evolves over 24 months, fitting vocabulary and scaling parameters on future time periods means the model sees the statistical distribution of future employee behavior during training. This is especially consequential for text-based features where vocabulary drift over time is expected.
defender_raw:
This RNN vs. logistic regression employee turnover experiment makes a genuinely important methodological choice that many HR analytics papers fail to make: the temporal boundary between observation window and prediction window is explicit, strict, and deployment-realistic. By defining the observation window as T=0 to T=720 days and the prediction window as T=720 to T=810, the design ensures that all communication behavior is constructed strictly before the outcome period. This prevents the most common and most damaging form of leakage in retention models, where communication changes during the departure process (reduced engagement, farewell messages) contaminate feature construction.

The RNN receives 26 timesteps of 2-week rolling statistics, while the logistic regression receives static aggregates over the full observation window. This is not giving the RNN an unfair advantage — it is the correct asymmetric design for testing whether temporal modeling adds value over static summaries. If both models received the same features, the comparison would test model class without isolating the temporal signal. The design correctly asks: does capturing the trajectory of communication behavior improve prediction beyond knowing the overall level?

Stratifying by department and employment tenure in the split ensures that the test set reflects the organization's actual structural composition. This matters because turnover rates vary substantially across departments and tenure levels; a split that fails to stratify could accidentally produce an easy test set dominated by high-turnover junior employees. The bidirectional LSTM with 64 hidden units is a reasonable architecture — not overparameterized for a 26-timestep sequence with 5-6 features — and focal loss or class-weighted cross-entropy is the appropriate response to the class imbalance inherent in turnover prediction.

The choice of ROC-AUC as the primary metric provides a threshold-independent evaluation that respects the reality that HR intervention thresholds change over time with policy and budget constraints. Precision is tracked as a secondary metric to ensure the RNN's recall advantage does not come at the cost of overwhelming intervention workflows.
all_issues_raised: Feature asymmetry confound: RNN receives 26-timestep temporal sequences while LR receives only static aggregates — architecture and information advantages conflated
Stratified random split on temporal employee data creates temporal leakage despite stated label boundary enforcement
Preprocessing transformers (scaling, tokenization) fit on entire dataset before splitting — future communication distribution leaks into training
No independent baseline that applies the same temporal features to a simpler model, making architectural contribution unidentifiable
Class imbalance handling with focal loss or weighted cross-entropy not matched to LR's class weight tuning — confounds model comparison
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on temporal employee data creates temporal leakage despite stated label boundary enforcement
Preprocessing transformers (scaling, tokenization) fit on entire dataset before splitting — future communication distribution leaks into training
